query: evidence for role of RNA polymerase in eukaryotic protein synthesis

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Polymerase -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Essential and fundamental to all organisms, transcription is the process for RNA synthesis from template DNA. At the heart of this activity is the large multisubunit enzyme called RNA polymerase. RNA polymerase, abbreviated RNAP and officially known as DNA-directed RNA polymerase, is found in all living organisms as well as many viruses. Present in bacteria, archaea, and even eukaryotes, these RNAPs all share similar protein core structures as well as mechanisms.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Replication and Transcription -- Molecular Level -- Transcription
retrieved_docs.content: RNA is synthesized from the DNA template by a process known as transcription. This requires the action of enzymes called RNA polymerases to generate a single-stranded RNA from one of the parent DNA strands. Unlike DNA polymerase, RNA polymerase can initiate RNA synthesis without a primer. While prokaryotes have a single RNA polymerase, eukaryotic cells possess three RNA polymerases. RNA synthesis requires RNA polymerase I for ribosomal RNA, RNA polymerase II for mRNA and microRNA, and RNA polymerase III for tRNA and other small RNAs. Transcription is not as accurate as replication and produces more errors. The replication process is highly accurate, due to the proofreading ability of DNA polymerase. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Primary Protein Structure -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: In any cell, the DNA preserves the code used to synthesize proteins. The nucleotide sequence of a protein-encoding gene is transcribed into another nucleotide sequence (RNA transcript or mRNA), which is then used to synthesize a sequence of amino acids to form a protein. This is historically known as the Central Dogma. RNA polymerases transcribe genes. In eukaryotes, there are 3 polymerases (I, II, III): RNA polymerase I transcribes genes for 18S, 5.8S, and 28S ribosomal RNA in the nucleus. RNA polymerase II transcribes encoding genes for messenger RNA (mRNA) and small RNAs. RNA polymerase III synthesizes 5S RNA, and all transfer RNA (tRNA); is located in the nucleoplasm. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Polymerase -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: RNAP is a highly abundant enzyme which catalyzes the formation of phosphodiester bonds linking nucleotides together, subsequently producing linear chain. The growing RNA chain is extended by one nucleotide at a time in the 5’ to 3’ direction using nucleoside triphosphates (ATP, CTP, UTP, and GTP) as substrates. Estimates are that about 20 nucleotides undergo synthesis for each gene with over a thousand transcripts formed in an hour from one gene. Unlike DNA polymerase, RNAP has increased infidelity. One mistake is made every 10,000 nucleotides while DNA polymerase makes one mistake every 10,000,000 nucleotides. Despite lower accuracy, RNAP does have a proofreading mechanism. RNAP can back up, excise the misincorporated ribonucleotide, and insert the proper ribonucleotide.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Molecular Level -- How is it made?
retrieved_docs.content: RNA polymerases synthesize RNA from DNA through a process called transcription. In prokaryotes, a single RNA polymerase catalyzes transcription for all types of RNA. In eukaryotes, there are different types of RNA polymerases, each responsible for synthesizing a specific RNA. RNA polymerase I synthesize rRNA. RNA polymerase II creates mRNA, and RNA polymerase III makes tRNA. To initiate transcription, an RNA polymerase enzyme binds to a promoter region on DNA, and the DNA double helix unwinds into a template strand and non-coding strand. During transcription, an RNA polymerase uses the 3’-5’ DNA template strand to synthesize a 5’-3’ RNA strand with complementary nucleotides. The newly synthesized RNA strand is nearly identical to the non-coding strand of DNA except for uracil substituting thymine. In eukaryotes, each RNA polymerase has a unique mechanism to terminate transcription. For example, RNA polymerase II transcribed RNA has an AAUAAA poly(A) site that recruits a group of factors to cleave the transcript. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Polymerase -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Along with prokaryotic transcription inhibition, researchers have discovered several compounds that target eukaryotic RNAP. Actinomycin D is a bacterial antibiotic used as an antitumor reagent. It intercalates DNA, thereby preventing the progression of both bacterial and eukaryotic RNAP I. [35] [36] Along with actinomycin D, other chemicals also inhibit eukaryotic transcription. Alpha-amanitin binds to the bridge helix and trigger loop of RNAP II and III, preventing the incorporation of nascent RNA chains, [37] [38] while 8-hydroxyquinoline chelates bivalent cations, manganese, and magnesium, within the active site of RNAP. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Polymerase -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: RNAP is the multisubunit enzyme that transcribes template DNA into RNA. While bacteria (prokaryotes) and archaea contain only one RNAP, eukaryotes contain three RNAPs: RNAP I, RNAP II, and RNAP III. Though there are drastic differences between these multisubunit RNAPs, they are also many significant similarities. Clearly related and forming a family, these RNAPs have three highly conserved subunits. Though archaea RNAP is more complex than bacterial RNAP and is more closely related to eukaryotic RNAP II, about 50% of the enzyme is still conserved. [1] Thus, scientists have been able to glean many of RNAP’s important structure and function by studying the simpler bacterial RNAP. Because bacterial RNAP is most heavily researched and has the most significant clinical relevance, most of this activity will focus on this RNAP.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Polymerase -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: To initiate the process of bacterial transcription, sigma and core together first binds to double-stranded DNA. [21] [2] C-terminal domains of the alpha subunits both interact with the UP elements, Region 4.2 interactions with the -35, and Region 3.0 interacts with the extended -10. [2] These interactions between core and promoter DNA are very unstable and short-lived, forming a product called the unstable closed complex (RPc). [21] The thinking is that the double-stranded DNA lies across the face of the RNAP. RPc quickly transitions to the stable open complex (RPo) where the DNA unzips, bends about 90 degrees, and forms a bubble from -11/12 to about +5 relative to the +1 transcriptional start site. [21] [22] Along with DNA changes, there are also major protein conformational changes within polymerase: Region 1.1 moves downstream to enter that beta and beta prime channel and beta prime clamp undergoes large conformational changes, allowing the downstream DNA to be fully secured. [22] Nucleotide triphosphates then enter through the secondary channel of the core, leading to the synthesis of RNA and forming a complex called the initiating complex (RPi). These RNA products are very short and are also known as abortives. Once the abortives reach a certain size, they can ultimately push Region 3.2 out of its current position within the RNA exit channel. [23] This change thus opens up the RNA exit channel, leading to the release of both the sigma factor as well as RNA. Core polymerase continues moving along the template stranded DNA until it reaches the termination site. In Rho-dependent termination, a factor called Rho is responsible for disrupting the complex of RNAP/RNA/template DNA while in Rho-independent termination, a loop forms at the end of the RNA molecule, allowing for RNAP to fall off. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Replication and Transcription -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Transcription is the process where a specific segment of DNA is used as a template and copied into an RNA molecule. This synthesis is carried out by an enzyme known as RNA polymerase. The newly synthesized RNA molecule then exits the nucleus and enters the cytoplasm, where it is translated into protein. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Replication and Transcription -- Molecular Level -- Replication
retrieved_docs.content: DNA polymerase then starts synthesizing the new strand from 5’ to 3’ direction, but requires a short RNA sequence, approximately 10 nucleotides in length, as a primer to begin the process. An RNA primer is therefore generated with the help of RNA primase enzyme, to initiate placing RNA bases complementary to the template strand bases. The DNA strands are directional with one side (3') denoted by a hydroxyl group and another represented by a phosphate group (5'). Multiple RNA primers are needed for the lagging strand which is then used by the DNA polymerase to begin the elongation phase of DNA replication. DNA replication is high fidelity due to the proofreading ability of DNA polymerase that detects, removes, and fixes any errors made during the replication process. [2] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Replication and Transcription -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Eukaryotic DNA replication occurs in the nucleus of a cell wherein new DNA is made using the original DNA as a template. The process occurs in three stages-initiation, elongation, and termination. Once the DNA is formed, it undergoes the process of transcription synthesizing messenger RNA, which will then be used to generate proteins. Similar to replication, transcription also follows a three-step process of initiation, elongation, and termination. mRNA formation is followed by post-transcriptional modifications in eukaryotic cells and forms the basis of gene expression.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Polymerase -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: RNAP’s function is to transcribe DNA into RNA. The template stranded DNA is read from 3’ to 5’ fashion while RNA synthesis occurs in a 5’ to 3’ manner. Many different kinds of RNA get produced: mRNA, tRNA, rRNA, sRNA, snoRNA, and other noncoding RNAs.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Replication and Transcription -- Molecular Level -- Transcription
retrieved_docs.content: Once the RNA polymerase is able to bind to the section of the gene that will undergo transcription, it continues to separate the double helix and synthesize RNA in a 5' to 3' direction. The RNA polymerase places complementary bases to the template strand, except instead of placing thymine with every adenine, the polymerase places a new base called uracil (U). This process is the elongation phase as the RNA polymerase continues down the template creating a new complementary single-stranded RNA. The elongation step proceeds until the polymerase meets a hairpin loop structure known as the termination sequence, which causes the polymerase to fall off, thus beginning the termination phase.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Protein Synthesis -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical significance of protein synthesis lies not only in human translation but in differences between human and bacterial translation. The bacterial ribosome (70S) has the same core components and many structurally similar sites compared to the eukaryotic ribosome (80S). However, translational differences between humans and bacteria create targets for antimicrobial drugs. These differences allow certain antibiotics to bind selectively to bacterial ribosomes at low concentrations, targeting bacteria selectively and either inhibiting growth or killing the microbe. Several commonly prescribed antibiotics target specific components of the bacterial ribosome and mRNA. Aminoglycosides target the 30S small ribosomal subunit; specifically, this class binds to the rRNA segment active in the A site. The tetracyclines operate similarly by competing for the A site with charged aminoacyl tRNA. The macrolide antibiotics act on the 50S large ribosomal subunit. When they bind to the rRNA of the large subunit, it prevents the formation of the peptide bond and promotes the early expulsion of the tRNA in the P site. [12] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: RNA viruses can survive and disrupt physiological processes by replicating their genome inside a host cell. There are different kinds of RNA viruses, and each has a unique mechanism of replication. Double-stranded RNA viruses, such as retroviruses, fuse with host cell membranes and inject their viral contents inside to replicate their genome via reverse transcription. Reverse transcription requires a ribonuclease H and a DNA polymerase with the ability to copy an RNA or a DNA template to synthesize a linear double-stranded DNA from an RNA. [19] Positive-stranded RNA viruses have a positive virion RNA that already acts as an mRNA and, therefore, can be translated immediately by RNA polymerase. Negative-stranded RNA viruses have a negative virion RNA that is complementary to mRNA and must be copied into a positive-sense mRNA before translation. Viral proteins are then released from the host cell to infect other cells, consequently interfering with normal biological processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1412
retrieved_docs.content: Increased protein synthesis is a physiologic effect associated with both mupirocin and nusinersen. Mupirocin, an antimicrobial agent, and nusinersen, an antisense oligonucleotide therapy, each promote the up‑regulation of protein production as part of their pharmacologic actions, highlighting a shared mechanistic outcome despite their different therapeutic indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1606
retrieved_docs.content: Mipomersen has a physiologic effect of decreasing protein synthesis, meaning that treatment with this antisense oligonucleotide leads to reduced synthesis of proteins in the body. This decrease in protein synthesis is a key aspect of mipomersen’s mechanism of action and contributes to its therapeutic and safety profile.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 626
retrieved_docs.content: Ganciclovir and cidofovir are both classified as members of the “Nucleosides and nucleotides excl. reverse transcriptase inhibitors” group. Within this class, ganciclovir’s mechanism of action is identified as “Nucleic Acid Synthesis Inhibitors [MoA],” meaning it exerts antiviral effects by inhibiting the synthesis of nucleic acids. By belonging to the same nucleoside/nucleotide category, both ganciclovir and cidofovir share structural and functional properties that target viral replication through disruption of nucleic acid synthesis, a key principle in antiviral drug therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1484
retrieved_docs.content: Ganciclovir is an antiviral agent whose mechanism of action is classified as a nucleic acid synthesis inhibitor, meaning it interferes with the synthesis of viral DNA and thereby suppresses viral replication. This pharmacologic property places ganciclovir within the broader category of nucleic acid synthesis inhibitors, highlighting its role in targeting viral replication processes and informing its use in the treatment of infections such as cytomegalovirus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: The class “Nucleic Acid Synthesis Inhibitors [MoA]” defines a mechanism of action that blocks viral nucleic‑acid synthesis, and several antiviral agents belong to this class. Ganciclovir is a member of the broader category “Nucleosides and nucleotides excl. reverse transcriptase inhibitors” and its antiviral effect is mediated through nucleic‑acid synthesis inhibition. The same class also includes cidofovir, brincidofovir, brivudine, and remdesivir, all of which act as nucleoside or nucleotide analogues that interfere with viral DNA or RNA polymerases. Brivudine is chemically mapped to bromodeoxyuridine. Remdesivir, as a member of this nucleoside/nucleotide group, may be used to treat viral infection (عدوى فيروسية). Ribavirin is noted for its ability to prevent viral infection. Brincidofovir and tecovirimat have demonstrated activity against smallpox (الجدري), a disease that is classified as a viral infection (has parent viral infection → الجدري). This interconnected network shows how agents sharing the nucleic‑acid synthesis inhibition mechanism are employed to treat or prevent a variety of viral diseases, ranging from common viral infections to specific orthopoxvirus illnesses such as smallpox, underscoring the importance of understanding their class membership, mechanisms, and therapeutic indications in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2080
retrieved_docs.content: Cidofovir is classified as a member of the pharmacologic group “nucleosides and nucleotides excluding reverse transcriptase inhibitors,” indicating that it belongs to the broader category of nucleoside and nucleotide analogues but does not function as a reverse transcriptase inhibitor. This classification helps differentiate cidofovir’s mechanism of action and therapeutic use from other antiviral agents that target reverse transcriptase.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1705
retrieved_docs.content: N [Preparations] serve as the parent class for the therapeutic agent nusinersen, indicating that nusinersen is a specific preparation within this broader category. Nusinersen, in turn, produces increased protein synthesis as a physiologic effect, reflecting its mechanism of action. Understanding that nusinersen belongs to the N [Preparations] family and that it induces increased protein synthesis helps clarify its role in clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 830
retrieved_docs.content: Cyclic amines, direct acting antivirals are classified as direct acting antivirals, indicating that this group of cyclic amine compounds belongs to the broader class of antiviral agents that directly inhibit viral replication by targeting specific viral enzymes or proteins.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 415
retrieved_docs.content: Ganciclovir is identified as a nucleic acid synthesis inhibitor, indicating that its mechanism of action involves disrupting the synthesis of viral nucleic acids. In addition, ganciclovir is classified as a member of the broader pharmacologic group “Nucleosides and nucleotides excl. reverse transcriptase inhibitors,” a category that also comprises the antiviral agents cidofovir and brivudine. This shared classification reflects common structural or functional characteristics among these drugs, despite their distinct individual uses. Brivudine, another member of this nucleoside/nucleotide class, is specifically mapped to the chemical entity bromodeoxyuridin, highlighting its molecular identity. Together, these relationships clarify how ganciclovir, cidofovir, and brivudine are related through their inclusion in the same drug class and, for ganciclovir, its specific role as a nucleic acid synthesis inhibitor, which is essential for understanding their antiviral mechanisms and guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1228
retrieved_docs.content: Cidofovir and brincidofovir are both classified as members of the pharmacologic group “Nucleosides and nucleotides excl. reverse transcriptase inhibitors.” This class comprises antiviral agents that act as nucleoside or nucleotide analogs but do not function as reverse transcriptase inhibitors, indicating that both cidofovir and brincidofovir share a similar mechanism of interfering with viral DNA synthesis and are used in the treatment of DNA virus infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 86
retrieved_docs.content: antiparasitic, and supportive pharmacologic agents, highlighting shared mechanisms such as decreased protein synthesis, overlapping chemical families (imidazole, triazole, nitroimidazole), and dual therapeutic roles that are essential for informed therapeutic decision‑making and optimized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 397
retrieved_docs.content: Actinomycin is chemically related to depsipeptidy, indicating that it belongs to or is associated with the broader class of depsipeptide compounds. In addition, Actinomycin functions as a DNA minor groove binding agent, placing it within the category of agents that specifically bind to the minor groove of DNA to interfere with transcription and replication processes. This dual characterization highlights both its structural classification as a depsipeptide‑related molecule and its pharmacological role in targeting DNA architecture.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1220
retrieved_docs.content: Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors constitute a pharmacologic class that includes delavirdin, efavirenz, etravirine and nevirapin. All four agents share the same mechanism of action, binding to a distinct allosteric site on the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme and thereby inhibiting viral DNA synthesis without requiring intracellular phosphorylation. Because they act at the same enzymatic target, these drugs are grouped together for clinical decision‑making in combination antiretroviral therapy, allowing clinicians to select appropriate members of the class based on potency, resistance profile, tolerability and drug‑interaction considerations. Their classification as members of the non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor class underscores their collective role in suppressing HIV replication and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1879
retrieved_docs.content: Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors constitute a class of antiretroviral agents that target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme by binding to a site distinct from the active catalytic region, thereby blocking viral RNA synthesis. Key members of this pharmacologic group include delavirdin, rilpivirine, etravirine, and nevirapine, each of which shares the common mechanism of non‑competitive inhibition of reverse transcriptase. By incorporating these drugs into combination antiretroviral therapy, clinicians can enhance viral suppression, reduce the risk of resistance development, and improve overall treatment outcomes for patients living with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2666
retrieved_docs.content: Delavirdin, rilpivirin, and nevirapin are all members of the pharmacologic class known as non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs). NNRTIs constitute a group of antiretroviral agents that inhibit the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme by binding to a distinct non‑nucleoside site, thereby preventing viral RNA transcription and replication. By sharing this common mechanism of action, delavirdin, rilpivirin, and nevirapin are used in combination regimens to treat HIV infection, offering an alternative to nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors and contributing to the overall effectiveness of antiretroviral therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 281
retrieved_docs.content: Rimantadine is classified as a cyclic amine and a direct‑acting antiviral, placing it within a group of compounds that target viral replication mechanisms. A notable physiologic effect of rimantadine is the alteration of cellular synthetic activity, which can influence both viral production and host cell metabolic processes. Recognizing rimantadine’s membership in the cyclic amine, direct‑acting antiviral class and its capacity to modify cellular synthesis helps clinicians understand its mechanism of action, anticipate potential cellular effects, and optimize its use in antiviral therapy.
